title: C Basics
description: Learn C from scratch — the foundation of modern programming. Master variables, control flow, functions, arrays, and pointers.

units.title: C Foundations
units.description: Understand how C works and write your very first C programs.

lessons.title: What is C?
lessons.description: Get to know C — a compiled, low-level language that powers operating systems and hardware.

exercises.title: How C runs
exercises.description: C is a compiled language — your source code goes through a compiler before it can run.
exercises.question: What must happen to a C program before it can run?
options: It must be compiled into an executable
It is interpreted line by line at runtime
It runs directly from the .c file
It compiles automatically when you open the file
exercises.explanation: C source code (.c) is compiled by a compiler (e.g., gcc) into a machine-code executable. Only then can it run.

exercises.title: C file extension
exercises.description: C source files use a specific extension.
exercises.question: C source code files use the .c extension (e.g., hello.c).
exercises.explanation: C source files end in .c. After compilation you get an executable file.

exercises.title: The main function
exercises.description: Every C program must have a specific entry point.
exercises.question: What is the entry point of every C program?
exercises.explanation: Every C program starts execution from int main(). The return type is int because main() returns an exit code to the OS.

exercises.title: Static typing
exercises.description: C handles types differently from Python.
exercises.question: In C, you must declare the type of every variable before using it (e.g., int x = 5;).
exercises.explanation: C is statically typed — you must declare int x, float y, char c, etc. The compiler checks types at compile time.

exercises.title: C file extension
exercises.description: Fill in the extension for C source files.
exercises.codeTemplate: // Save your C program as: my_program.___
hints: C source files use a single-letter extension.

lessons.title: Your First Program
lessons.description: Write and run your very first C program using printf.

exercises.title: printf function
exercises.description: C uses printf() to print output to the console.
exercises.question: Which function is used to print output in C?
exercises.explanation: printf() is the standard C function for printing output. It comes from the <stdio.h> header.

exercises.title: stdio.h header
exercises.description: To use printf, you need to include a specific header file.
exercises.question: Which header must you include to use printf()?
exercises.explanation: #include <stdio.h> brings in standard input/output functions like printf() and scanf().

exercises.title: Predict the output
exercises.description: Read this C program and predict what it prints.
hints: printf prints exactly the string inside the quotes (no newline unless you add \n).

exercises.title: Newline in printf
exercises.description: printf does not add a newline automatically.
exercises.question: printf() automatically adds a newline at the end of the output.
exercises.explanation: Unlike Python's print(), printf() does NOT add a newline. You must write \n explicitly: printf("Hello\n");

exercises.title: Hello, World!
exercises.description: Write a complete C program that prints `Hello, World!` exactly (no newline needed).
hints: Use printf() inside main().
printf("Hello, World!");

lessons.title: Comments and Structure
lessons.description: Learn how to structure a C file and add comments.

exercises.title: Single-line comment
exercises.description: C supports two styles of comments.
exercises.question: Which syntax is used for a single-line comment in C?
exercises.explanation: // starts a single-line comment. /* ... */ is used for multi-line comments.

exercises.title: return 0 in main
exercises.description: The return value of main() has a specific meaning.
exercises.question: Returning 0 from main() signals that the program ended successfully.
exercises.explanation: return 0; tells the operating system the program ran without errors. A non-zero value means an error occurred.

exercises.title: Include directive
exercises.description: Fill in the missing header include.
hints: You need the standard input/output header.

exercises.title: Predict the output
exercises.description: What does this program print?
hints: Comments are ignored. Only the printf runs.

exercises.title: Print with newline
exercises.description: Write a program that prints `Learning C` on one line followed by a newline, so the output is exactly: `Learning C\n`.
hints: Use \n inside the string to add a newline.
printf("Learning C\n");

lessons.title: Chapter 1 Review
lessons.description: Review: C foundations, printf, headers, and program structure.

exercises.title: C program entry point
exercises.description: Recall how a C program starts.
exercises.question: Where does execution of a C program begin?
exercises.explanation: int main() is the mandatory entry point of every C program.

exercises.title: Compiled language
exercises.description: Recall whether C is compiled or interpreted.
exercises.question: C is an interpreted language — it runs source code directly without compiling.
exercises.explanation: C is a compiled language. The compiler (e.g., gcc) translates .c source code into a machine-code executable.

exercises.title: printf call
exercises.description: Fill in the function call to print Hi.
hints: The standard C output function.

exercises.title: Predict output
exercises.description: What does this program print?
hints: Two printf calls print back to back with no newline between them.

exercises.title: Print your name
exercises.description: Write a complete C program that prints exactly: `My name is C`
hints: Use printf() with the exact string.
printf("My name is C");

units.title: Variables and Data Types
units.description: Declare and use variables. Understand C's core data types: int, float, char, double.

lessons.title: Integer Variables
lessons.description: Declare and use int variables in C.

exercises.title: Declaring an int
exercises.description: In C, you must declare a variable's type before using it.
exercises.question: How do you declare an integer variable named age with value 20?
exercises.explanation: int age = 20; declares an integer variable. The type (int) comes first, then the name, then the value.

exercises.title: Printing an int
exercises.description: printf uses format specifiers to print different types.
exercises.question: Which format specifier prints an int with printf?
exercises.explanation: %d is the format specifier for integers. Example: printf("%d", age);

exercises.title: Predict the output
exercises.description: What does this program print?
hints: %d prints the integer value of x.

exercises.title: Declare an int
exercises.description: Fill in the type keyword.
hints: The keyword for whole-number integers in C.

exercises.title: Print an integer
exercises.description: Declare an int variable `score` with value 42 and print it. Expected output: `42`
hints: Use %d as the format specifier.
printf("%d", score);

lessons.title: Float and Double
lessons.description: Work with decimal numbers using float and double.

exercises.title: Decimal types
exercises.description: C has two main types for decimal numbers.
exercises.question: Which format specifier prints a float with printf?
exercises.explanation: %f is used to print float values. %lf is for double in scanf, but printf accepts %f for both.

exercises.title: float vs double
exercises.description: float and double both store decimal numbers but differ in precision.
exercises.question: A double uses more memory than a float but stores decimal numbers with more precision.
exercises.explanation: float is 4 bytes (~6-7 digits precision). double is 8 bytes (~15-16 digits). Use double for most math.

exercises.title: Predict the output
exercises.description: What does this print? (%.1f rounds to 1 decimal place)
hints: %.1f formats a float to 1 decimal place. 3.14 rounded to 1 decimal is 3.1.

exercises.title: Float format specifier
exercises.description: Fill in the format specifier for a float.
hints: The format specifier for floating-point numbers.

exercises.title: Print a float
exercises.description: Declare a float variable `temp` with value 36.6 and print it to 2 decimal places. Expected output: `36.60`
hints: Use %.2f to print 2 decimal places.
printf("%.2f", temp);

lessons.title: Characters and Strings
lessons.description: Use char for single characters and char arrays for strings.

exercises.title: char type
exercises.description: C has a specific type for single characters.
exercises.question: How do you declare a char variable named grade with value A?
exercises.explanation: Single characters use single quotes: char grade = 'A';. Double quotes are for strings.

exercises.title: Printing a char
exercises.description: printf has a specific specifier for characters.
exercises.question: Which format specifier prints a char?
exercises.explanation: %c prints a single character. Example: printf("%c", grade);

exercises.title: Predict the output
exercises.description: What does this print?
hints: %c prints the character value stored in letter.

exercises.title: Single vs double quotes
exercises.description: In C, single and double quotes have different meanings.
exercises.question: In C, single quotes are for single characters and double quotes are for string literals.
exercises.explanation: 'A' is a char. "A" is a string (a char array). They are different types — mixing them causes warnings or errors.

exercises.title: Print a character
exercises.description: Declare a char variable `initial` with value `M` and print it. Expected output: `M`
hints: Use single quotes for char literals.
printf("%c", initial);

lessons.title: Chapter 2 Review
lessons.description: Review: int, float, double, char, and printf format specifiers.

exercises.title: Format specifiers
exercises.description: Match the type to its printf format specifier.
exercises.question: Which format specifier is correct for printing an int?
exercises.explanation: %d for int, %f for float/double, %c for char, %s for strings.

exercises.title: Static typing
exercises.description: Recall C's type system.
exercises.question: In C, you can use a variable without declaring its type first.
exercises.explanation: C requires you to declare the type before using a variable: int x; float y; char c;

exercises.title: Predict the output
exercises.description: What does this print?
hints: 3 + 7 = 10. %d prints the integer result.

exercises.title: Char declaration
exercises.description: Fill in the type for a character variable.
hints: The C type for a single character.

exercises.title: Two variables
exercises.description: Declare `int x = 5` and `int y = 3`, then print their sum. Expected output: `8`
hints: Use printf("%d", x + y);

units.title: Operators and Expressions
units.description: Use arithmetic, comparison, and logical operators to compute and compare values.

lessons.title: Arithmetic Operators
lessons.description: Add, subtract, multiply, divide, and find remainders in C.

exercises.title: Modulus operator
exercises.description: C has an operator to find the remainder of division.
exercises.question: What does 10 % 3 evaluate to in C?
exercises.explanation: % is the modulus (remainder) operator. 10 / 3 = 3 remainder 1, so 10 % 3 = 1.

exercises.title: Integer division
exercises.description: Dividing two ints in C truncates the decimal part.
exercises.question: In C, 7 / 2 evaluates to 3 (integer division truncates toward zero).
exercises.explanation: When both operands are int, C performs integer division. 7 / 2 = 3, not 3.5. Use float/double to get decimals.

exercises.title: Predict the output
exercises.description: What does this print?
hints: 15 / 4 = 3 remainder 3. So 15 % 4 = 3.

exercises.title: Remainder operator
exercises.description: Fill in the operator to get the remainder.
hints: The modulus operator is a percent sign.

exercises.title: Arithmetic
exercises.description: Declare `int a = 20` and `int b = 6`, then print `a % b`. Expected output: `2`
hints: printf("%d", a % b);

lessons.title: Comparison and Logical Operators
lessons.description: Compare values and combine conditions with &&, ||, and !.

exercises.title: Comparison operators
exercises.description: C uses specific symbols for comparisons.
exercises.question: Which operator checks if two values are equal in C?
exercises.explanation: == checks equality (two equal signs). = is assignment. Using = instead of == is a common bug.

exercises.title: Logical AND
exercises.description: C uses && for logical AND.
exercises.question: What is the result of (5 > 3 && 2 < 4) in C?
exercises.explanation: Both conditions are true, so the && (AND) returns 1. In C, true is 1 and false is 0.

exercises.title: Predict the output
exercises.description: What does this print? (In C, true prints as 1, false as 0.)
hints: 10 > 5 is true. In C, true evaluates to 1.

exercises.title: Boolean values in C
exercises.description: C does not have a native bool keyword (without stdbool.h).
exercises.question: In C, comparisons return 1 for true and 0 for false.
exercises.explanation: C uses int for boolean logic: 1 = true, 0 = false. The stdbool.h header adds true/false keywords, but 1/0 is the underlying representation.

exercises.title: Print a comparison
exercises.description: Print the result of `3 != 5`. Expected output: `1` (because 3 is not equal to 5, which is true).
hints: Use %d to print the boolean result (1 or 0).

lessons.title: Increment and Assignment
lessons.description: Use ++, --, +=, and -= to update variables efficiently.

exercises.title: Increment operator
exercises.description: C has shorthand operators to increase a variable by 1.
exercises.question: What does x++ do?
exercises.explanation: x++ (post-increment) adds 1 to x. It is equivalent to x = x + 1.

exercises.title: Predict the output
exercises.description: What does this print?
hints: x starts at 5. x++ adds 1, making it 6.

exercises.title: Decrement
exercises.description: Fill in the decrement operator.
hints: The decrement operator is two minus signs.

exercises.title: Predict the output
exercises.description: What does this print?

exercises.title: Increment and print
exercises.description: Declare `int count = 7`, then increment it by 1, then print it. Expected output: `8`
hints: Use count++ to increment.
printf("%d", count);

lessons.title: Chapter 3 Review
lessons.description: Review: arithmetic, comparison, logical, and increment operators.

exercises.title: Integer division
exercises.description: Recall how integer division works.
exercises.question: What is the value of 9 / 2 in C when both are int?
exercises.explanation: Integer division truncates: 9 / 2 = 4 (remainder discarded).

exercises.title: Assignment vs equality
exercises.description: Common C bug: using = instead of ==.
exercises.question: In C, = is for assignment and == is for equality comparison.
exercises.explanation: = assigns a value. == compares two values. Using = in an if condition assigns instead of comparing — a classic C bug.

exercises.title: Predict the output
exercises.description: What does this print?
hints: x *= 3 multiplies x by 3. 4 * 3 = 12.

exercises.title: Modulus
exercises.description: Fill in the result of the modulus operation.
hints: 13 = 2*5 + 3. The remainder is 3.

exercises.title: Compute and print
exercises.description: Declare `int a = 100` and `int b = 7`, then print `a % b`. Expected output: `2`

units.title: Control Flow
units.description: Make decisions with if/else and switch. Control the path your program takes.

lessons.title: if and else
lessons.description: Use if and else to branch your program based on conditions.

exercises.title: if syntax
exercises.description: The if statement checks a condition.
exercises.question: Which is the correct if statement syntax in C?
exercises.explanation: C requires the condition in parentheses: if (condition) { ... }. No 'then' keyword.

exercises.title: Predict the output
exercises.description: What does this print?
hints: x is 10, which is greater than 5, so the if branch runs.

exercises.title: if condition
exercises.description: Fill in the condition.
hints: The variable to check is age.

exercises.title: else if
exercises.description: C supports chaining conditions with else if.
exercises.question: In C, you can chain multiple conditions using else if.
exercises.explanation: if (...) { } else if (...) { } else { } — you can chain as many else if blocks as needed.

exercises.title: Positive check
exercises.description: Declare `int n = 15`. If n > 0, print `positive`. Otherwise print `non-positive`. Expected output: `positive`
hints: Use if (n > 0) { printf("positive"); }

lessons.title: switch Statement
lessons.description: Use switch to branch based on exact integer or char values.

exercises.title: switch syntax
exercises.description: switch is used for multi-way branching on a value.
exercises.question: What keyword ends each case in a switch statement to prevent fall-through?
exercises.explanation: break exits the switch block. Without it, execution 'falls through' to the next case — usually a bug.

exercises.title: Predict the output
exercises.description: What does this print?
hints: day is 2, so case 2 matches and prints Tue.

exercises.title: default case
exercises.description: switch has a catch-all option.
exercises.question: The default case in a switch runs when no other case matches.
exercises.explanation: default is like an else for switch — it runs if no case value matches the switch expression.

exercises.title: break keyword
exercises.description: Fill in the keyword to exit a switch case.
hints: Without this, execution falls through to the next case.

exercises.title: switch on char
exercises.description: Declare `char grade = 'B'`. Use switch: if A print `Excellent`, if B print `Good`, default print `Other`. Expected output: `Good`
hints: switch works on char values too.
case 'B': printf("Good"); break;

lessons.title: Nested Conditions
lessons.description: Combine if/else and logical operators for complex decisions.

exercises.title: Logical AND in conditions
exercises.description: Use && to require both conditions to be true.
exercises.question: Which condition checks that x is between 1 and 10 (inclusive)?
exercises.explanation: && (AND) requires both conditions true. x >= 1 AND x <= 10 covers the range [1, 10].

exercises.title: Predict the output
exercises.description: What does this print?
hints: x=5 satisfies both x>0 and x<10, so both sides of && are true.

exercises.title: OR condition
exercises.description: Fill in the OR operator.
hints: The logical OR operator in C.

exercises.title: NOT operator
exercises.description: C uses ! to negate a condition.
exercises.question: In C, !0 evaluates to 1 (true) and !1 evaluates to 0 (false).
exercises.explanation: ! is the logical NOT operator. !0 = 1, !1 = 0. It inverts the boolean value.

exercises.title: Range check
exercises.description: Declare `int score = 75`. If score >= 50 AND score <= 100, print `pass`. Otherwise print `fail`. Expected output: `pass`
hints: Use && to combine two conditions.
if (score >= 50 && score <= 100)

lessons.title: Chapter 4 Review
lessons.description: Review: if/else, switch, break, and logical operators.

exercises.title: if without braces
exercises.description: When the if body has only one statement, braces are optional.
exercises.question: In C, if the body of an if statement is a single statement, the curly braces are:
exercises.explanation: Braces are optional for single-statement if bodies. But it's good practice to always use them to avoid bugs.

exercises.title: Predict the output
exercises.description: What does this print?
hints: In C, 0 is false (falsy). Any non-zero value is true.

exercises.title: switch default
exercises.description: Fill in the keyword for the catch-all case.
hints: The keyword that matches when no case matches.

exercises.title: Fall-through
exercises.description: A common switch bug.
exercises.question: If you forget break in a switch case, C will execute the next case's code automatically.
exercises.explanation: This is called 'fall-through'. Without break, C continues executing the next case body. Always use break unless fall-through is intentional.

exercises.title: Even or odd
exercises.description: Declare `int n = 9`. If n % 2 == 0, print `even`. Otherwise print `odd`. Expected output: `odd`
hints: Use n % 2 to check even/odd.
if (n % 2 == 0) is the even check.

units.title: Loops
units.description: Repeat code with for, while, and do-while loops.

lessons.title: for Loop
lessons.description: Use the for loop to repeat code a fixed number of times.

exercises.title: for loop structure
exercises.description: The for loop has three parts in its header.
exercises.question: What are the three parts of a C for loop header?
exercises.explanation: for (init; condition; update) { } — e.g., for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++) where i=0 is init, i<5 is condition, i++ is update.

exercises.title: Predict the output
exercises.description: What does this print?
hints: i goes 1, 2, 3. Each iteration prints i with no space or newline.

exercises.title: Loop condition
exercises.description: Fill in the condition to loop 5 times (i = 0 to 4).
hints: The loop variable is i.

exercises.title: Loop variable scope
exercises.description: Variables declared inside a for loop header.
exercises.question: A variable declared in the for loop header (e.g., int i = 0) is only accessible inside the loop.
exercises.explanation: In C99 and later, int i = 0 in a for header limits i's scope to the loop body. After the loop ends, i is no longer accessible.

exercises.title: Count up
exercises.description: Use a for loop to print the numbers 1 to 5 each separated by a space. Expected output: `1 2 3 4 5`
hints: Loop from i=1 to i<=5.
Print a space before each number except the first.

lessons.title: while Loop
lessons.description: Use while to repeat as long as a condition is true.

exercises.title: while vs for
exercises.description: while is best when you don't know how many iterations you need.
exercises.question: When is a while loop preferred over a for loop?
options: When the number of iterations is not known in advance
When you always repeat exactly N times
When you need a loop index
When you need to count from 0
exercises.explanation: Use while when you loop until a condition changes (e.g., reading user input, processing a list). Use for for counted iterations.

exercises.title: Predict the output
exercises.description: What does this print?
hints: n starts at 1, increments each iteration: 1, 2, 3. Loop stops when n > 3.

exercises.title: Infinite loop prevention
exercises.description: Fill in the update to avoid an infinite loop.
hints: You need to increment i so the condition eventually becomes false.

exercises.title: while false
exercises.description: What if the while condition is false from the start?
exercises.question: If the while condition is false before the loop starts, the loop body never executes.
exercises.explanation: while checks the condition before each iteration. If false on the first check, the body runs zero times.

exercises.title: Count down
exercises.description: Use a while loop to print `3 2 1` (space-separated). Expected output: `3 2 1`
hints: Start at n=3, decrement, stop when n < 1.
Print a space before each number except the first.

lessons.title: break and continue
lessons.description: Use break to exit a loop early, and continue to skip an iteration.

exercises.title: break in a loop
exercises.description: break can be used inside loops, not just switch.
exercises.question: What does break do inside a loop?
exercises.explanation: break exits the innermost loop immediately. Execution continues after the loop's closing brace.

exercises.title: Predict the output
exercises.description: What does this print?
hints: i goes 1, 2, then 3 triggers break. So only 1 and 2 are printed.

exercises.title: continue keyword
exercises.description: continue skips the rest of the current iteration.
exercises.question: What does continue do inside a loop?
options: Skips the rest of the current iteration and moves to the next
Starts the loop over from the beginning
exercises.explanation: continue skips the remaining code in the current iteration and goes to the loop's update/condition check.

exercises.title: Predict the output
exercises.description: What does this print? (skips even numbers)
hints: continue skips even numbers (2 and 4). Odd numbers 1, 3, 5 are printed.

exercises.title: Skip multiples of 3
exercises.description: Loop from 1 to 6. Skip any number divisible by 3. Print the rest. Expected output: `1245`
hints: Use if (i % 3 == 0) continue;
Numbers: 1,2,4,5 are not divisible by 3.

lessons.title: Chapter 5 Review
lessons.description: Review: for loop, while loop, break, and continue.

exercises.title: Loop choice
exercises.description: Choosing between for and while.
exercises.question: Which loop is best for iterating exactly 10 times?
exercises.explanation: for is the natural choice for a known iteration count: for (int i = 0; i < 10; i++). It keeps init, condition, and update together.

exercises.title: Predict the output
exercises.description: What does this print?

exercises.title: break statement
exercises.description: Fill in the statement to exit the loop when i equals 3.
hints: The keyword to immediately exit a loop.

exercises.title: do-while
exercises.description: do-while always executes the body at least once.
exercises.question: A do-while loop checks the condition after executing the body, so it always runs at least once.
exercises.explanation: do { ... } while (condition); — the body runs first, then the condition is checked. Guaranteed minimum 1 execution.

exercises.title: Sum with for
exercises.description: Use a for loop to compute the sum of integers 1 through 5 and print it. Expected output: `15`
hints: Declare sum=0 before the loop.
Add i to sum each iteration.

units.title: Functions and Arrays
units.description: Write reusable functions and store multiple values in arrays.

lessons.title: Functions
lessons.description: Define and call functions to organize and reuse code.

exercises.title: Function syntax
exercises.description: A C function declaration specifies return type, name, and parameters.
exercises.question: How do you define a function that takes an int and returns an int?
exercises.explanation: C functions: returnType name(paramType paramName) { ... }. Example: int square(int n) { return n * n; }

exercises.title: Predict the output
exercises.description: What does this print?
hints: double_it(5) returns 5*2=10.

exercises.title: void functions
exercises.description: Functions that return nothing use void.
exercises.question: What return type do you use for a function that prints but returns nothing?
exercises.explanation: void means the function returns no value. You can still use return; (no value) to exit early, or just let it reach the closing brace.

exercises.title: Return value
exercises.description: Fill in the keyword to return a value from a function.
hints: The keyword that sends a value back to the caller.

exercises.title: Write a function
exercises.description: Write a function `int square(int n)` that returns n*n. In main, print `square(4)`. Expected output: `16`
hints: Define square before main.
return n * n;

lessons.title: Arrays
lessons.description: Declare and access arrays to store multiple values of the same type.

exercises.title: Array declaration
exercises.description: Arrays in C store a fixed number of same-type elements.
exercises.question: How do you declare an int array of 5 elements named nums?
exercises.explanation: int nums[5]; declares an array of 5 ints. Access elements with nums[0] through nums[4].

exercises.title: Array indexing
exercises.description: C arrays are zero-indexed.
exercises.question: What is the index of the first element in a C array?
exercises.explanation: C arrays are zero-indexed: arr[0] is the first element, arr[1] is the second, etc. arr[n-1] is the last of an n-element array.

exercises.title: Predict the output
exercises.description: What does this print?
hints: nums[1] is the second element, which is 20.

exercises.title: Array access
exercises.description: Fill in the index to access the third element.
hints: Arrays are zero-indexed. Third element is at index 2.

exercises.title: Sum array elements
exercises.description: Declare `int arr[4] = {2, 4, 6, 8}`. Use a for loop to compute and print the sum. Expected output: `20`
hints: Loop from i=0 to i<4.
sum += arr[i] inside the loop.

lessons.title: Function Parameters and Scope
lessons.description: Understand how values are passed to functions and how scope works in C.

exercises.title: Pass by value
exercises.description: C passes arguments by value by default.
exercises.question: In C, when you pass an int to a function, what happens?
options: A copy of the value is passed; the original is unchanged
exercises.explanation: C passes by value: the function gets a copy. Modifying the parameter inside the function does NOT change the original variable.

exercises.title: Predict the output
exercises.description: What does this print?
hints: add_ten gets a copy of x. Modifying n inside doesn't affect x. x stays 5.

exercises.title: Local scope
exercises.description: Variables declared inside a function are local to that function.
exercises.question: A variable declared inside a function cannot be accessed outside that function.
exercises.explanation: Local variables only exist within the { } block where they're declared. Once the function returns, those variables are gone.

exercises.title: Function prototype
exercises.description: Fill in the return type for a function that returns an int.
hints: The function returns an integer value.

exercises.title: Function with two params
exercises.description: Write a function `int multiply(int a, int b)` that returns a*b. Call it with 6 and 7 and print the result. Expected output: `42`
hints: return a * b;

lessons.title: Chapter 6 Review
lessons.description: Review: functions, arrays, pass by value, and scope.

exercises.title: Array bounds
exercises.description: C does not check array bounds at runtime.
exercises.question: What happens if you access arr[10] on an array of size 5 in C?
options: Undefined behavior — C does not check bounds
An error is thrown at runtime
The program crashes with a clear error message
exercises.explanation: C does NOT bounds-check arrays. Accessing out-of-bounds memory causes undefined behavior — it may crash, corrupt data, or appear to work.

exercises.title: Predict the output
exercises.description: What does this print?
hints: 3 > 7 is false, so b (7) is returned.

exercises.title: Array size
exercises.description: Fill in the size to declare an array of 10 ints.
hints: The size goes inside the square brackets.

exercises.title: void return
exercises.description: void functions and return.
exercises.question: A void function must not have any return statement at all.
exercises.explanation: void functions can use bare return; (without a value) to exit early. What they can't do is return a value.

exercises.title: Find the maximum
exercises.description: Declare `int arr[5] = {3, 1, 9, 2, 7}`. Loop through it and find and print the maximum value. Expected output: `9`
hints: Start with max = arr[0].
Loop and update max if arr[i] > max.
